author: Augustine of Hippo
book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.

ccel.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

source.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

title: Psalm CL

source.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Although the arrangement of the Psalms, which seems to me to contain the secret of a mighty mystery, hath not yet been revealed unto me, yet, by the fact that they in all amount to one hundred and fifty, they suggest somewhat even to us, who have not as yet pierced with the eye of our mind the depth of their entire arrangement, whereon we may without being over-bold, so far as God giveth, be able to speak.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Firstly, the number fifteen, whereof it is a multiple this number fifteen, I say, signifieth the agreement of the two Testaments.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For in the former is observed the Sabbath, which signifieth rest; in the latter the Lord’s Day, which signifieth resurrection.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The Sabbath is the seventh day, but the Lord’s Day, coming after the seventh, must needs be the eighth, and is also to be reckoned the first.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For it is called the first day of the week, and so from it are reckoned the second, third, fourth, and so on to the seventh day of the week, which is the Sabbath.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But from Lord’s Day to Lord’s Day is eight days, wherein is declared the revelation of the New Testament, which in the Old was as it were veiled under earthly promises.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Further, seven and eight make fifteen.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Of the same number too are the Psalms which are called “of the steps,” because that was the number of the steps of the Temple.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Further too, the number fifty in itself also containeth a great mystery.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For it consisteth of a week of weeks, with the addition of one as an eighth to complete the number of fifty.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For seven times seven make forty-nine, whereto one is added to make fifty.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And this number fifty is of so great meaning, that it was after the completion of that number of days from the Lord’s Resurrection, that, on the fiftieth day exactly, the Holy Spirit came upon those who were gathered together in Christ.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And this Holy Spirit is in Scripture especially spoken of by the number seven, whether in Isaiah or in the Apocalypse, where the seven Spirits of God are most directly mentioned, on account of the sevenfold operation of one and the self-same Spirit.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And this sevenfold operation is mentioned in Isaiah.…Hence also the Holy Spirit is spoken of under the number seven.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But this period of fifty the Lord divided into forty and ten: for on the fortieth day after His Resurrection He ascended into heaven, and then after ten days were completed He sent the Holy Spirit: under the number forty setting forth to us the period of temporal sojourn in this world.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For the number four prevaileth in forty; and the world and the year have each four parts; and by the addition of the number ten, as a sort of reward added for the fulfilment of the law in good works, eternity itself is figured.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This fifty the number one hundred and fifty containeth three times, as though it were multiplied by the Trinity.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Wherefore for this reason too we make out that this number of the Psalm is not unsuitable.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now in that some have believed that the Psalms are divided into five books, they have been led by the fact, that so often at the end of Psalms are the words, “so be it, so be it.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But when I endeavoured to make out the principle of this division, I was not able; for neither are the five parts equal one to another, neither in quantity of contents, nor yet even in number of Psalms, so as for each to contain thirty.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And if each book end with, “so be it, so be it,” we may reasonably ask, why the fifth and last book hath not the same conclusion.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: We however, following the authority of canonical Scripture, where it is said, “For it is written in the book of Psalms,” know that there is but one book of Psalms.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And I see indeed how this can be true, and yet the other be true also, without contravening it.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For it may be that there was some custom in Hebrew literature, whereby that is called one book which yet consists of more than one, just as of many churches one church consisteth, and of many heavens one heaven,…and one land of many lands.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For it is our everyday habit to say, “the globe of the earth,” and “the globe of the lands.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And when it is said, “It is written in the book of Psalms,” though the customary way of speaking is such that he seem to have wished to suggest that there is but one book, yet to this it may be answered, that the words mean “in a book of the Psalms,” that is, “in any one of those five books.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And this is in common language so unprecedented, or at least so rare, that we are only convinced that the twelve Prophets made one book, because we read in like manner, “As it is written in the book of the Prophets.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: There are some too who call all the canonical Scriptures together one book, because they agree in a very wondrous and divine unity.…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Whichever then of these is understood, this book, in its parts of fifty Psalms each, gives an answer important and very worthy of consideration.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For it seems to me not without significance, that the fiftieth is of penitence, the hundredth of mercy and judgment, the hundred and fiftieth of the praise of God in His saints.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For thus do we advance to an everlasting life of happiness, first by condemning our own sins, then by living aright, that, having condemned our ill life, and lived a good life, we may attain to everlasting life.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Our predestination is not wrought in ourselves, but in secret with Him, in His foreknowledge.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But we are called by the preaching of repentance.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: We are justified in the calling of mercy and fear of judgment.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He feareth not judgment, who hath previously attained salvation.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Being called, we renounce the devil by repentance, that we may not continue under his yoke: being justified, we are healed by mercy, that we may not fear judgment: being glorified, we pass into everlasting life, where we praise God without end.…The verse wherewith this Psalm concludeth is the voice of life everlasting.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Praise the Lord in His saints,” that is, in those whom He hath glorified: “praise Him in the firmament of His power” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Praise Him in His deeds of strength;” or, as others have explained it, “in His deeds of power: praise Him according to the multitude of His greatness” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: All these His saints are; as the Apostle saith, “But we may be the righteousness of God in Him.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: If then they be the righteousness of God, which He hath wrought in them, why are they not also the strength of Christ which He hath wrought in them, that they should rise again from the dead?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For in Christ’s resurrection, “strength” is especially set forth to us, for in His Passion was weakness, as the Apostle saith.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And well doth it say, “the firmament of His power.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For it is the “firmament of His power” that He “dieth no more, death hath no more dominion over Him.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Why should not they also be called “the works of” God’s “strength,” which He hath done in them: yea rather, they themselves are the works of His strength; just as it is said, “We are the righteousness of God in Him.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For what more powerful than that He should reign for ever, with all His enemies put under His feet?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Why should not they also be “the multitude of His greatness”? not that whereby He is great, but whereby He hath made them great, many as they are, that is, thousands of thousands.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Just as righteousness too is understood in two ways, that whereby He is righteous, and that which He worketh in us, so as to make us His righteousness.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: These same saints are signified by all the musical instruments in succession, to praise God in.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For what the Psalmist began with, saying, “Praise the Lord in His saints,” that he carrieth out, signifying in various ways these same saints of His.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Praise Him in the sound of the trumpet” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 3): on account of the surpassing clearness of note of their praise.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Praise Him in the psaltery and harp.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The psaltery praiseth God from things above, the harp praiseth God from things below; I mean, from things in heaven, and things in earth, as He who made heaven and earth.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: We have already in another Psalm, explained that the psaltery hath that board, whereon the series of strings rests that it may give a better sound, above, whereas the harp has it below.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Praise Him in the timbrel and choir” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The “timbrel” praiseth God when the flesh is now changed, so that there is in it no weakness of earthly corruption.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For the timbrel is made of leather dried and strengthened.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The “choir” praiseth God when society made peaceful praiseth Him.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Praise Him on the strings and organ.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Both psaltery and harp, which have been mentioned above, have strings.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But “organ” is a general name for all instruments of music, although usage has now obtained that those are specially called organ which are inflated with bellows: but I do not think that this kind is meant here.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For since organ is a Greek word, applied generally, as I have said, to all musical instruments, this instrument, to which bellows are applied, is called by the Greeks by another name: but it being called organ is rather a Latin and conversational usage.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: When then he saith, “on the strings and organ,” he seemeth to me to have intended to signify some instrument which hath strings.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For it is not psalteries and harps only that have strings: but, because in the psaltery, and harp, on account of the sound from things below and things above, somewhat has been found which can be understood after this distinction, he hath suggested to us to seek some other meaning in the strings themselves: for they too are flesh, but flesh now set free from corruption.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And to those, it may be, he added the organ, to signify that they sound not each separately, but sound together in most harmonious diversity, just as they are arranged in a musical instrument.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For even then the saints of God will have their differences, accordant, not discordant, that is, agreeing, not disagreeing, just as sweetest harmony arises from sounds differing indeed, but not opposed to one another.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Praise Him on the well-sounding cymbals, praise Him on cymbals of jubilation” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Cymbals touch one another in order to sound, and therefore are by some compared to our lips.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But I think it better to understand that God is in a manner praised on the cymbal, when each is honoured by his neighbour, not by himself, and then honouring one another, they give praise to God.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But lest any should understand such cymbals as sound without life, therefore I think he added, “on cymbals of jubilation.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For “jubilation” that is, unspeakable praise, proceedeth not, save from life.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Nor do I think that I should pass over what musicians say, that there are three kinds of sounds, by voice, by breath, by striking: by voice, uttered by throat and windpipe, when man singeth without any instrument; by breath, as by pipe, or anything of that sort: by striking, as by harp, or anything of that kind.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: None then of these kinds is omitted here: for there is voice in the choir, breath in the trumpet, striking in the harp, representing mind, spirit, body, but by similitudes, not in the proper sense of the words.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: When then he proposed, “Praise God in His saints,” to whom said he this, save to themselves?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And in whom are they to praise God, save in themselves?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For ye, saith he, are “His saints;” ye are “His strength,” but that which He wrought in you; ye are “His mighty works, and the multitude of His greatness,” which He hath wrought and set forth in you.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Ye are “trumpet, psaltery, harp, timbrel, choir, strings, and organ, cymbals of jubilation sounding well,” because sounding in harmony.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: All these are ye: let nought that is vile, nought that is transitory, nought that is ludicrous, be here thought of.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And since to savour of the flesh is death, “let every spirit praise the Lord” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Prayer of St. Augustin.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Which he was wont to use after his Sermons and Lectures.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Turn we to the Lord God, the Father Almighty, and with pure hearts offer to Him, so far as our meanness can, great and true thanks, with all our hearts praying His exceeding kindness, that of His good pleasure He would deign to hear our prayers, that by His Power He would drive out the enemy from our deeds and thoughts, that He would increase our faith, guide our understandings, give us spiritual thoughts, and lead us to His bliss, through Jesus Christ His Son our Lord, who liveth and reigneth with Him, in the Unity of the Holy Spirit, one God, for ever and ever.
